speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4 of our interview with Mr Hyman Skulsky. Tape 4. So you get a job with the British consulate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So first of all, there were-- I don't know how many people applied for this job. So she said, leave it to me, she said. You'll get the job. She was in with the British consul, with this Mr. Fell. And she got me the job. I put in my application. Then a week later, I was called up for an interview, and I got the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was quite-- I had a very interesting job. I used to drive all kinds of vehicles. Sunday-- Saturday, I didn't work. Sunday, every Sunday, I drove the British ambassador. This was Sunday. His driver was off, so I drove him. In the morning, I drove him to church. In the afternoon, sometimes, to someplace to visit. But it was a-- for me, it couldn't have been a nicer job.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I used to take people on tours with an Israeli guide. The guide was-- they were always known people, not just anybody, an officer in the army or-- and he used to explain to the British-- whoever was in the car-- tell them the history of every place. And I used to sit there and listen. I enjoyed it. It couldn't have been any nicer for me. And I worked there for four, four and a half, five years almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always as a driver?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a driver. And I enjoyed it. And many a time, I could take my car home. Not always, but-- not over weekends, for instance. But during the week, many a time, I could take the car home. So it gave me a chance to do things for myself. And by the way, my little bungalow had arrived, so-- with a lift, with all the things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, finally, I found a group of people who all had done the same. The only thing, my bungalow was the smallest. People bought houses from Sweden, from Germany, from Italy, proper homes, pre-fabricated, with three bedrooms, four bedrooms. And we all got together at the [NON-ENGLISH] not the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us a piece of-- each one of us got a piece of land next to each other in the place called Yas'ur or Mishmar HaShiv'a which is very close to Tel Aviv. And they had people build-- put their houses up. And I used to go on weekends. Whenever I had time, or even after work, I used to go there. And except for the foundation-- I had to have people put the foundation in-- but the rest I put up myself. It's a one-room bungalow, living room with a bedroom, a kitchen, and a bathroom. And, in fact, it's still standing there. It's not mine anymore. But it's standing there. And I lived there for five years, rent free, on my own. And I used to-- I made a lot of friends there in this little town. It used to be an old Arab town years ago. And the Arabs had left there. They all ran away. And there were a lot of old buildings there. I mean, you know how the Arabs lived, their buildings, very old buildings. And now they're building there a lot. But anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: What prompted you to want to then leave the embassy? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the embassy. I left Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: To leave the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: To leaves the-- no, I didn't. I would have been-- stayed on with the job. But I wanted to go to America. I had my two brothers living here-- not here. In New York, they both lived in New York. And Sam, the one that lives in Beverly Hills, had come to New York with his wife and son in 1950. They settled down there, and everything was all right until his wife got cancer. And she suffered for one year. She-- he had no insurance, and she died in New York, a very young woman. And he was left penniless practically in New York. And I always used to be with him in business, so I decided I want to come to be with him. So when I left, I came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in New York in April, 1954. April, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, for the first year-- almost year, I lived with my brother in Kew Gardens. Kew Gardens-- Kew Garden Hills! Kew Garden Hills.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Queens?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Queens. And then he found another wife, very nice person. And they got married, so I moved out. I took my own home. I rented a home in Kew Gardens this time, not far from him. And three years later--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I worked for-- in the watch business. Somebody whom I knew in Belgium and in Israel before the war, or during the war-- no, after the war, I met him. He had a big business in watches in New York. He used to import movements from Switzerland, import cases from Hong Kong, and bands from Japan, and used to assemble them, cheap-- very cheap line of watches. And he used to sell them all over the country. And when he saw me, he said, you've got to work for me. So I said, good. I didn't make much. But, anyway, I had a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked there for three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In his place, I worked about three or four years, till we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my brother, Sam, with his wife, he decided he was fed up of New York. He didn't like New York anymore. So he said he is going to California to look around. So he came here, and he liked-- he loved it. He loved Los Angeles right away. So about a couple months later, or a month later, his wife joined him. And that was it. He was here, and we were there. And two years after he had come here, we decided to come here for Pesach, to be-- to visit him for Pesach. So we came in 1961 for Pesach, to be with him. And we fell in love with the place. We came from New York where they had several times 17 inches of snow and freezing cold. And we loved it here. So two months later, we moved out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my wife, Meta, in New York on a blind date. Somebody gave me her number. And it was in August, I believe, or September. And this was our busiest time in the business. So I put the number in my pocket. And then my sister-in-law was the [NON-ENGLISH]. So she kept on asking me. I said, I'm too busy now. At the end of the year, when business slows down, I'll call her. And so it was. At the end of the year, when business was very slow, I called Meta on a blind date. And we met. And we hit it off. And I think I was very lucky with her. And a few months later, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: On May the 3rd, 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: '59. And then you moved out to Los Angeles with your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I moved out-- in '61, we moved out to Los Angeles, to Los Angeles with her. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, who always-- he wanted to go into business again, in the lighting, like we used to have in Belgium, the lighting business. So we started together in partnership. And we ran the business till 1985. The last business we had was in Pacific Palisades. And we had a business here for 17 years in the same spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the place was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selectra Creations. We had two shops. One was lighting, mostly lighting-- it was all lighting. And the other one, we had bathroom accessories, towels, soap dishes, little shelves, everything for the bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've lived in Los Angeles since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before-- yes, since '61.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you retired when?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1985, We retired, 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish up, Mr. Skulsky is there anything else you would want to say about your experiences or about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, the worst part of everything, the war and after the war, was finding out what happened to our relatives. Because when we lived in Antwerp, I had a big family, lots of uncles, aunts, first cousins, and friends. And most of them were killed by Hitler. Most of them were sent to Auschwitz and Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of their Belgian--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were Russians. Most of them were Russians. Some of them were Belgian. The only ones that were saved-- the only one was me because I was born in England. And this was all a book, a little passport, a little blue book that saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were a British national?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in London, yeah. And I've never changed my nationality. And I never will.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're still a British subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm still a British subject.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you very much for taking the time. And now, if it's OK with you, we would like to meet your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Meta, would you like to come in here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Skulsky, would you introduce us to this lovely woman next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely wife, Meta. And as I say, we've been married since '59. That's 36 years. And I'm very happy with her. And I hope to have many more years together with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've been married for double Chai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Double Chai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, we thank you for taking the time. And we wish you from now on many, many more years of health and happiness together. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now if it's OK with you, Mr. Skulsky, we'll take a few minutes and photograph some of the pictures that are in your remembrances.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'll do that now. Mr. Skulsky, will you tell us who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my whole family, my mother, my father, my two brothers, my sister, and I. This is my mother and my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Rosa Skulsky, Sylvie Skulsky, and Lazar Skulsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. My late brother, Abraham Skulsky, my brother Sam Skulsky. And that's myself, Hyman Skulsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Antwerp around 1923 or 1922, about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture, Mr. Skulsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture are my two brothers and I, taken in London in probably around 1918. This is me, Hyman; my oldest brother, Abraham; and my other brother, Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, nothing

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us who's in this picture, Mr. Skulsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, Hyman Skulsky. And this was taken after I was arrested by the Germans. And it was put on the German identification card, which they kept until the end of the war when we went into their offices, and every one took their own identification card back from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kreuzberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us about the inscription in the upper right-hand corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: The inscription in red ink in the upper right-hand corner says Jude, which means Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also here, it says Jude.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, where it says Jude in black, this they would do with anybody. If he's a different-- has a different religion or whatever, they would put it in for anybody. But the red mark was to show-- not to forget that they have a Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture, Mr. Skulsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: This again is my own picture, Hyman. And it's the last identity card I had during the war in Belgium. And it says-- it has a red stamp on it, which every Jew had to get on his identity card. And it says Jood, which means Jew in Flemish, and Juif, which means Jew in French.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Antwerp at the beginning of the war-- during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940, yeah. I think it was '41 because that's when I got the identity card, I believe. '41. 16th of November, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's 1943 then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's valid till-- the card was valid until the 16th of November 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture, Mr. Skulsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the last camp in Spittal an der Drau on the day that the war finished. And this is a ceremony in the camp when the Union Jack was going to be hoisted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say May the 8th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Spittal is in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria. Spittal is in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my friend, prisoner-- ex-prisoner, Paul Sulnick, who now lives in South Africa, and myself, Hyman Skulsky, taken just before-- maybe on the day the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the right, and my friend Paul is on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture, Mr. Skulsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken by Mr. Zerteberg, who represented the YMCA. And it was the funeral of one of the prisoners that died just before the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this picture was taken in Spittal an der Drau in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately mid-- beginning of May 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: In beginning of May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my wife's wedding and my own, [LAUGHS] and was taken in Brooklyn, New York on May the 3rd, 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once again, Mr. Skulsky, on behalf of the survivors of the Shoah Foundation, we thank you very much for your time. And we wish you nothing but extreme happiness in the future. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.
